attributes.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to hospital for an elective laparoscopic total hysterectomy. She has a medical history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes, for which she takes perindopril and metformin. She is a non-smoker and drinks three glasses of wine at weekends. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 134/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Her body mass index (BMI) is 28 kg/m2. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable. She is counselled that mechanical compression devices will be used during the surgical procedure to reduce the risk of venous thromboembolism.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate pharmacological agent for post-operative thromboprophylaxis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient requires post-operative thromboprophylaxis due to multiple risk factors, including age over 60, BMI over 25 kg/m2, and major surgery. Risk assessment tools help guide the choice and duration of prophylaxis by balancing thrombotic and bleeding risks.

Enoxaparin is the most appropriate choice as it is the standard of care for post-operative thromboprophylaxis. Low molecular weight heparins have predictable pharmacokinetics, do not require monitoring, and have extensive evidence supporting their use in this setting.

Aspirin alone is not recommended for post-operative thromboprophylaxis as it is less effective than low molecular-weight heparin.

Warfarin is not suitable for immediate post-operative prophylaxis due to its delayed onset of action, need for monitoring, and unpredictable anticoagulant effect in the immediate post-operative period.

Apixaban is an option for thromboprophylaxis post-knee or total hip replacement only.

Heparin infusion is used for therapeutic anticoagulation. Additionally, continuous intravenous heparin requires frequent monitoring and dose adjustments, making it impractical for routine prophylaxis in this setting.

The duration of prophylaxis should be continued until the patient is fully mobile, typically 5-7 days for uncomplicated laparoscopic surgery. Extended prophylaxis may be considered for patients with additional risk factors or more extensive surgery.
learningPoints: Low molecular weight heparin is the preferred agent for post-operative venous thromboprophylaxis in most surgical patients.
Duration of thromboprophylaxis should be tailored to individual patient risk factors and type of surgery.
Risk assessment tools help guide thromboprophylaxis decisions by balancing thrombotic and bleeding risks.
references: Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care. Venous Thromboembolism Prevention  Clinical Care Standard  [Internet]. Clinical Care Standard. 2018 [cited 2022 Dec 6]. Available from: https://www.safetyandquality.gov.au/standards/clinical-care-standards/venous-thromboembolism-prevention-clinical-care-standard

Pannucci CJ, Swistun L, MacDonald JK, Henke PK, Brooke BS. Individualized Venous Thromboembolism Risk Stratification Using the 2005 Caprini Score to Identify the Benefits and Harms of Chemoprophylaxis in Surgical Patients. Annals of Surgery. 2017 Jun;265(6):1094–103.

Nelson G, Bakkum-Gamez J, Kalogera E, et al. Guidelines for perioperative care in gynecologic/oncology: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) Society recommendations—2019 update. Int J Gynecol Cancer 2019; 0:1–18.
